runs.content:
**Investigation idea:** GlobalWafers flagged as a plausible new Tier 3 watchlist candidate but never actually added anywhere. ai-tech/source-micron-us-chipmaking-2026-07-09.md's Investment Angle section identifies GlobalWafers (6488.TWO, Taiwan-listed) as a candidate on the newly-identified raw-silicon-wafer chokepoint (5-player oligopoly, >90% combined share) — the page itself says this is 'pending further diligence on its listing accessibility and financials,' but that diligence was never done. It appears in no formal investment-signals table and no Finance idea/watchlist page. Next step: check broker accessibility for GlobalWafers ADRs/OTC shares, then add it to the constraint-edges table as Tier 3 or note why it's excluded.

**Cross-domain connection 1:** Fred's own live trading portfolio holds two direct bets on the AI capex thesis, with none of AI & Tech's or Finance's own bear-case research ever mentioned against them. finance/trading-portfolio.md holds real-money positions in Amphenol (APH, 'AI data centre demand') and Broadcom (AVGO, 'AI networking + custom chips') — both bought on the AI infrastructure buildout thesis AI & Tech has extensively documented. But neither the trading portfolio nor the daily portfolio-management-briefing ever cites the wiki's own bear case on the same thesis — Grantham's AI-bubble call, Klement's 'AI capex ROI negative for all hyperscalers except Amazon,' or Clapham's Buffett-vs-SoftBank piece. The briefing only ever runs bullish/neutral technical signals on these two names.

**Cross-domain connection 2:** The token-pricing deflation thesis in AI & Tech is a direct operating-cost risk to Fred's own Crypto holding, unexamined. ai-tech/source-arora-token-pricing-2026-07-09.md has Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora saying LLM token costs must fall ~90% within two years for enterprise AI economics to work, with Palantir's Karp warning high token costs already push users to cheaper substitutes. crypto/company-everything-inc.md describes Everything.inc's core differentiator as Eva, a 'code-execution-based financial AI agent' managing the protocol's pool — almost certainly running on paid LLM inference. Fred holds $EV, yet neither page ever asks whether Eva's own token/inference costs are sustainable at DeFi-protocol fee scale, or whether Eva is a real running agent vs. a marketing label (the company page's own unresolved Key Questions literally ask 'what exactly does Eva do'). Sharper and more specific than the general AI-capex-bubble-extends-to-Crypto connection logged 2026-07-05.

**Investigation idea:** Games' prototype scaffold exists only in a memory note, never reached the actual wiki page. memory/index.md's Domain Content Status for Games says '4 prototype stubs in raw/games/: Mobile Wood Chopping Game, Children's Financial Literacy Game (+Lightfin crossover !todo), Crypto Portfolio Tracker, Project Planning Interface' — but raw/games/ doesn't currently exist as a folder, and games/prototypes.md's table is still the single placeholder '[to be added]' row it's had since 2026-05-01. The four prototype names and their one-line descriptions are sitting nowhere except that memory line. Next step: check with Fred whether those four files were ever created (and later moved/deleted) or the memory note is simply stale; either way, transcribe the four descriptions into games/prototypes.md's table.

**Cross-domain connection 1:** Finance already has the fully-built Kelly Criterion page that Sports Betting's own bankroll-management stub is explicitly waiting for. sports-betting/bankroll-management.md lists 'Kelly Criterion — optimal bet size given edge and odds' and 'Fractional Kelly' as the top two items under 'Key Concepts (to be built out),' unchanged since 2026-05-01. finance/concepts/concept-kelly-criterion.md already has the full f*=(bp−q)/b formula, a documented Half-Kelly practice (~75% volatility reduction for ~25% growth cost), and a full mispricing trade-process checklist — built out 2026-05-26 from book-a-man-for-all-markets. Neither page references the other. The Sports Betting stub doesn't need new research to close this section; it needs a translation pass on content the wiki already owns.

**Cross-domain connection 2:** Moneyball is the one book in the library that's actually about Sports Betting's stated mission, and it's filed only under Finance. book-moneyball.md's own 'Why Notable' section frames the book as being about 'finding value where others aren't looking' — exploiting a market's statistical mispricing of talent — and cross-references only Finance overview and Investment ideas. sports-betting/overview.md's stated focus is 'using AI and data-driven models to find edge in sports betting markets... systematic, model-informed betting,' close to a direct restatement of Beane/DePodesta's approach one domain over. Sports Betting has never referenced the one book in the wiki that's the closest real-world analog to what the domain is trying to build.
